evidence: Birth strongly resonates with temporal.emergence (beginning of life), temporal.transformation (fundamental change from non-existence to existence), and symmetry.generative (creation of life). It contrasts with EXS.death's 'permanent cessation' through these slots. High scores also reflect its material/structural/semantic grounding, catalytic/chained interconnections, and sacred value in cultural narratives.

Scores peak in temporal.permanence (10), ontological.material (10), resonance.essential/existential (10), symmetry.destructive (10), and lawfulness.predictable (10). These reflect brain death's definitive, irreversible end of life, its physical basis, and its existential significance. Lower scores reflect its individual scale, diagnostic identification, and ethical implications.

Cell death strongly resonates with existential permanence (end of existence) and destructive symmetry (as the opposite of life). It operates at an individual cellular scale and is understood through scientific identification/understanding. Its necessity in biological processes and functional value in medicine are central.

Cerebral death directly relates to the permanent cessation of brain function (temporal.permanence 10, resonance.existential 10). It operates at an individual scale (scale.individual 10) and material layer (ontological.material 10). Its diagnosis relies on sensory data (epistemological.perception 2) and medical identification (epistemological.identification 10). It has ethical implications (value_generation.ethical 10) and practical utility (value_generation.functional 10).

Cot death (SIDS) strongly resonates with temporal transformation/permanence (permanent end of life), scale.individual (affects one infant), epistemological.identification/experience (named condition and lived grief), ontological.material/semantic (physical death with societal meaning), interconnection.independent/catalytic (tragic event triggering change), resonance.essential/existential (core of death's finality), symmetry.destructive (sudden loss), lawfulness.emergent/contingent (unpredictable but context-dependent), and value_generation.ethical (moral implications of infant mortality).

Crib death strongly resonates with permanence, materiality, and destructive symmetry as it represents a sudden, definitive end of life. It connects to individual scale, identification/experience (medical diagnosis and family impact), and ethical implications due to its tragic, unexplained nature.

The word 'dead' strongly resonates with permanence, individual scale, material ontology, and existential/essential resonance as it directly denotes the end of life. It aligns with destructive symmetry (as the opposite of life) and ethical value due to its moral implications. Scores reflect its core connection to the cessation of existence and related conceptual layers.

Deadness strongly resonates with existential permanence (resonance.existential 10) and the irreversible end of existence (temporal.permanence 10). It directly relates to individual life's cessation (scale.individual 10) and the material/semantic layers of being (ontological.material/semantic 10 each). Perception and identification of death (epistemological.perception/identification 10) anchor its experiential and ethical dimensions (experience.comprehension 10, value_generation.ethical 10).

Death resonates strongly with permanence (temporal), individual scale, material ontology, essential/existential resonance, destructive symmetry, and necessary lawfulness. These reflect its core role as an irreversible end to life and existence, contrasting directly with its symmetric pair (life).

Decease strongly resonates with permanence (10) as the permanent cessation of life, material existence (10), and existential resonance (10) as an end of existence. It contrasts with life via destructive symmetry (10). Ethical and sacred values (5 each) reflect its moral and transcendent implications. Catalytic interconnection (5) highlights its impact on others, while transformation (5) marks its fundamental change in existence.

Decease strongly resonates with existential and essential resonance (death as core to existence), symmetry.destructive (direct opposition to life), and scale.individual (ending a single life's existence). It also connects to permanence (permanent cessation) and necessary lawfulness (inevitable finality). Ethical and sacred value scores reflect death's moral and transcendent implications.

The word 'defunct' strongly resonates with permanence (death's finality), semantic meaning (labeling cessation), and existential resonance (core of existence's end). It weakly connects to individual scale (commonly applied to entities), structural ontology (organizational dissolution), and necessary lawfulness (inevitability of death).

Defunctness resonates strongly with temporal.permanence (permanent cessation), symmetry.destructive (opposite of life), resonance.essential/existential (core essence of non-existence), and ontological.material/semantic (physical and meaning layers). Scores drop elsewhere due to the word's focus on finality rather than process or interconnectedness.

Depart resonates most strongly with existential and sacred dimensions of death (resonance.existential=3, value.sacred=4), its inevitability (lawfulness.necessary=4), and its role in transforming relationships (symmetry.transformative=3). It also ties to the semantic meaning of death as departure (ontological.semantic=3) and the existential permanence of death (temporal.permanence=3).

Departure strongly resonates with existential and essential resonance (death as a core, fundamental end), symmetry.destructive (as death's finality), and permanence in time. It ties to individual scale, relational impacts, and ethical/sacred value due to its metaphorical use in describing death as a final leaving. Scores are grounded in the word's euphemistic role in framing death as an irreversible departure from existence.

The word 'die' strongly resonates with existential, destructive, and permanent cessation themes. It directly ties to individual scale, material reality, and ethical implications. Scores reflect its role as a fundamental transformation (temporal), inevitable outcome (lawfulness), and core existential end (resonance). Secondary scores reflect relational impacts, perceptual recognition, and transformative dynamics.

The phrase 'eternal rest' strongly resonates with permanence, existential essence, and sacred value, as it directly evokes death's finality and transcendent meaning. It aligns with individual scale and necessary lawfulness, reflecting death's inevitability and independence from external factors.

The phrase strongly evokes permanence (temporal.permanence 10) and existential finality (resonance.existential 10). It directly maps to the individual scale (scale.individual 10) and operates at the semantic meaning layer (ontological.semantic 10). As the opposite of life, it embodies destructive symmetry (symmetry.destructive 10) and the necessary inevitability of death (lawfulness.necessary 10). The metaphor identifies death as a state akin to sleep (epistemological.identification 5).

The word 'exit' strongly resonates with existential and essential resonance (touching the core essence of existence's end), destructive symmetry (as the opposite of life), necessary lawfulness (inevitability), material ontology (physical cessation), semantic meaning (conceptual significance), and ethical/sacred value (moral and transcendent implications). Temporal aspects highlight transformation and permanence as key features.

The verb 'exit' in the context of death strongly resonates with existential and essential resonance (core of existence), symmetry.destructive (as the opposite of life), and permanence (permanent cessation). It also connects to material ontology (physical death), individual scale (single life's end), and chained interconnection (death as the final cause-effect outcome).

Expiration directly embodies the permanent end of existence (temporal.permanence), operates at an individual scale (scale.individual), and is deeply experiential (epistemological.experience). It fundamentally alters material existence (ontological.material) and touches the core essence (resonance.essential) and existential nature (resonance.existential) of death. As the opposite of life (symmetry.destructive), it is an inevitable (lawfulness.necessary) and independent (interconnection.independent) phenomenon.

Expire strongly resonates with existential and essential resonance (5), destructive symmetry (5), and necessary lawfulness (5), as death is a permanent, irreversible end. It also aligns with material ontology (5), individual scale (5), and catalyzes relational impacts (3). Lower scores reflect its direct connection to physical cessation and ethical/sacred implications.

Expiry as EXS.death strongly resonates with temporal.permanence (permanent end), scale.individual (applies to individuals), symmetry.destructive (opposite of life), resonance.existential (fundamental to existence), and ontological.material (physical cessation). Scores of 8-10 reflect core aspects of death's essence, inevitability, and existential impact.

The word 'extinct' strongly resonates with temporal.permanence (10) and resonance.essential/existential (10/9), reflecting the irreversible end of existence. It aligns with symmetry.destructive (10) as the antithesis of life, and ontological.material (8) for physical extinction. Scale.ecosystem/stellar (8/7) and interconnection.chained (3) highlight its systemic and cascading impacts. All other connections are negligible or contextually irrelevant.

Extinction strongly resonates with existential and destructive dynamics (resonance.existential:9, symmetry.destructive:9), as it signifies the permanent end of existence (temporal.permanence:7). It operates at ecosystem scale (scale.ecosystem:7) and relates to ethical value (value_generation.ethical:8). Scores reflect its core essence (resonance.essential:7) and dependency on conditions (lawfulness.contingent:6).

Fatality strongly resonates with permanence (EXS.death's core definition), destructive symmetry (as the opposite of life), and existential resonance (end of existence). It scores highly in material ontology (physical cessation), individual scale (commonly referring to single deaths), and ethical value (moral implications of loss). Scores for chained interconnection (cause-effect sequences leading to death) and structural lawfulness (inevitability) reflect contextual usage.

Gangrene resonates strongly with temporal.transformation (as it signifies irreversible tissue death), ontological.material (physical decay), and symmetry.destructive (pathological process leading to death). It weakly connects to scale.individual (affects body parts), epistemological.identification (medical diagnosis), and value_generation.ethical (moral implications of neglect).

Gangrenous resonates strongly with temporal progression (influence, transformation), physical material decay, and existential resonance (essential/existential). It directly ties to destructive symmetry and ethical implications of bodily decay leading to death.

The verb 'go' in the context of death (e.g., 'He went to sleep') evokes permanence (temporal.permanence 5), existential resonance (resonance.existential 5), and inevitability (lawfulness.necessary 5). It ties to individual experience (epistemological.experience 3) and material cessation (ontological.material 3). The destructive symmetry (symmetry.destructive 5) reflects death's opposition to life's generative force. Minor scores reflect euphemistic naming (epistemological.identification 2) and semantic meaning (ontological.semantic 2).

The word 'going' (as a noun) strongly resonates with existential resonance (5) and destructive symmetry (5), as death marks an irreversible, fundamental end. It also aligns with permanence (5) and necessity (5), reflecting death's inevitability. The material (3) and experiential (4) aspects highlight the physical and lived reality of death, while sacred value (3) underscores its transcendent significance.

The highest scores reflect death's permanence (temporal.permanence), existential nature (resonance.existential), material reality (ontological.material), inevitability (lawfulness.necessary), and ethical implications (value_generation.ethical). Lower scores acknowledge its relational impact and contingent causes, while most slots remain 0 as the term focuses on the core cessation of life.

Infant death strongly resonates with existential and destructive themes (resonance.existential=10, symmetry.destructive=10), material reality (ontological.material=10), and ethical implications (value_generation.ethical=10). It transforms relationships (ontological.relational=8) and persists across scales (scale.individual=8, scale.community=7). Lower scores reflect its lack of connection to cosmic scales, creation processes, or superficial resonances.

The word 'kill' strongly resonates with EXS.death's core meaning: it directly causes the permanent cessation of life (temporal.permanence, temporal.transformation). It operates at the individual scale (scale.individual) as a material act (ontological.material) and embodies destructive symmetry (symmetry.destructive). Its existential resonance (resonance.existential) reflects the fundamental end of existence, while ethical implications (value_generation.ethical) are central to its use.

Lifeless strongly resonates with permanence (death's permanence), individual scale (end of an individual's life), material ontology (physical absence of life), destructive symmetry (opposite of life), and existential/essential resonance (core of existence). Ethical value arises from death's moral weight.

The word 'live' (adj) strongly resonates with EXS.death's opposite (EXS.life). It highlights life's permanence (temporal.permanence), continuation (temporal.continuation), and material/semantic essence (ontological.material/semantic). It identifies life's existence (epistemological.identification/experience) and embodies existential/sacred value (resonance.existential, value_generation.sacred). Its generative symmetry (symmetry.generative) contrasts death's destructive nature.

Loss resonates most strongly with existential and essential resonance (death's core essence), permanence (death's finality), relational ontology (loss of relationships), ethical value (moral implications of loss), and necessary lawfulness (inevitability of death). Scores reflect the profound, enduring impact of death-related loss on individuals, communities, and meaning systems.

The martyr's death is inherently tied to meaning (ontological.semantic 10) and ethical value (value_generation.ethical 10). Their influence persists (temporal.permanence 7) and transforms contexts (symmetry.transformative 10). The act catalyzes change (interconnection.catalytic 8) and resonates existentially (resonance.existential 7) as a sacrifice for a cause.

Martyrdom strongly resonates with individual sacrifice (scale.individual), ethical and sacred value (value.ethical, value.sacred), its semantic meaning (ontological.semantic), and transformative impact (symmetry.transformative). It weakly connects to societal influence (scale.society), experiential understanding (epistemological.experience), and contingent circumstances (lawfulness.contingent).

Megadeath strongly resonates with destructive symmetry (mass destruction), operates at societal scale, and aligns with the essential/existential aspects of death. It carries ethical implications and reflects material reality.

Mortification strongly resonates with existential and destructive themes tied to death's permanence and opposition to life. It connects deeply to individual scale, material reality, and ethical/sacred dimensions. Scores reflect its core role in marking the end of existence and its profound resonance in meaning and value.

Mortify (in the death context) strongly resonates with existential finality (resonance.existential), destructive force (symmetry.destructive), ethical implications (value_generation.ethical), and transformative change (symmetry.transformative). It operates primarily at the individual scale (scale.individual) and material layer (ontological.material).

Necrobiosis strongly resonates with EXS.death through its permanent, irreversible nature (temporal.permanence), destructive pathology (symmetry.destructive), and material manifestation in tissue (ontological.material). It involves fundamental tissue transformation (temporal.transformation) and operates at an individual scale (scale.individual). The essential resonance reflects its core role as a localized form of death.

Necrosis strongly resonates with temporal.permanence (permanent tissue death), temporal.transformation (fundamental cellular breakdown), and symmetry.destructive (pathological cell death). It operates at scale.individual (cellular level) and ontological.material (physical decay). Scores for interconnection.catalytic and lawfulness.contingent reflect necrosis's role as a consequence of damaging conditions.

Necrotic strongly resonates with material and destructive aspects of death (ontological.material:10, symmetry.destructive:10). It reflects permanent tissue death (temporal.permanence:5) and operates on an individual scale (scale.individual:8). Weak links exist to structural breakdown (ontological.structural:2), causal spread (interconnection.chained:3), and ethical implications (value_generation.ethical:2).

Neonatal death directly signifies an individual's permanent cessation of life (individual 10, material 10, existential 10). It is a named medical phenomenon (identification 10) with ethical implications (ethical 10). It opposes life (destructive 10) and may depend on medical conditions (contingent 5, predictable 7). Secondary scores reflect indirect community impact (community 2) and data categorization (informational 5).

The word 'out' strongly resonates with existential resonance and destructive symmetry as it denotes the end of existence (EXS.death). It scores highly in permanence (temporal) and individual scale, reflecting death's finality and personal nature. Sacred value underscores its transcendent significance.

The phrase 'out of play' strongly resonates with permanence (temporal.permanence), existential termination (resonance.existential), and the destructive opposition to life (symmetry.destructive). It also aligns with the inevitability of death (lawfulness.necessary) and its ethical/sacred valence. Lower scores reflect weaker or indirect connections to other slots.

The verb 'pass' in death contexts (e.g., 'pass away') strongly resonates with existential resonance (core of existence's end), necessary lawfulness (inevitability), and individual scale (personal nature of death). It also connects to destructive symmetry (opposite of life) and semantic ontology (euphemism for transition).

The word 'passing' strongly resonates with temporal.permanence (death's finality), ontological.semantic (its existential meaning), and resonance.existential (touching core existence). Scores reflect death's individual scale, destructive symmetry, and necessary lawfulness. Lower scores reflect minimal connections to other slots.

Quietus directly resonates with permanence, necessity, and existential/essential resonance as the definitive end of existence. It aligns with individual scale, material ontology, semantic meaning, and destructive symmetry (as life's opposite). The term's sacred value reflects its transcendent association with mortality.

The word 'release' in the context of death strongly resonates with existential and essential resonance (7/8), reflecting the core essence of ending existence. It connects to permanence (8), material release (5), and catalyzing change (3) in others. Ethical and sacred value scores (4/6) reflect moral and transcendent dimensions of death's finality.

The word 'rest' strongly resonates with permanence (death's finality) and existential essence (end of existence). It also ties to individual scale (e.g., 'rest in peace'), destructive symmetry (opposite of life), and necessary lawfulness (inevitability of death). Ethical and sacred undertones reflect cultural framing of death as a peaceful, transcendent state.

SIDS strongly resonates with ontological.material (physical death), scale.individual (affects single infants), and symmetry.destructive (sudden, unexplained loss). It also aligns with permanence (permanent cessation) and existential resonance (end of existence). Lower scores reflect its lack of connection to communal/social scales, predictive patterns, or aesthetic/sacred value.

Sleep as a metaphor for death strongly resonates with permanence (temporal.permanence), semantic meaning (ontological.semantic), and essential resonance (resonance.essential). It also aligns with destructive symmetry (symmetry.destructive) and necessary lawfulness (lawfulness.necessary), reflecting death's permanence and inevitability. Weaker ties include individual scale, experiential understanding, and sacred value.

Sphacelate refers to necrotic tissue death, strongly resonating with permanence (permanent cessation), transformation (fundamental tissue decay), destructive symmetry (pathological end state), and necessary lawfulness (inevitable progression).

Sphacelus (gangrene/necrosis) directly relates to the permanent cessation of tissue life (EXS.death). It strongly resonates with temporal.permanence (8), scale.individual (10), ontological.material (10), symmetry.destructive (10), and resonance.existential (10). Scores reflect its physical, irreversible nature and its role as a destructive pathological process.

Scores highest in temporal slots (suddenness, permanence), scale.individual (affects infants), symmetry.destructive (pathological tragedy), and existential resonance (core essence of death). Ethical value arises from societal implications of infant safety.

Wrongful death resonates most strongly with ethical value, permanence in time, and the semantic layer's moral judgment. It also connects to individual scale, relational impacts, and destructive symmetry as a harmful event.
